intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: People intimidated by cooking respond better to a single dominant metric (calories, prep time, or servings) than to dense nutritional tables or ingredient lists.
facade.label: One big number
facade.acceptImplies: Users want information hierarchy—lead with what matters most, bury complexity. Recipe cards should prioritize ONE scannable stat.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want transparency and options upfront. Dense tables and complete ingredient lists are preferable to reductive single metrics.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer approachable, non-intimidating UI that feels forgiving and low-stress
facade.label: Soft rounded buttons
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use rounded, cushioned interactions and soft visual language to reduce friction for cooking-averse users
facade.rejectImplies: Sharp, angular, or minimal UI may feel sterile or demanding—this audience needs visual reassurance, not sleekness

facade.hypothesis: For people intimidated by cooking, a warm, inviting atmosphere reduces friction and makes the app feel supportive rather than demanding
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: Design should lean into approachable, human, slightly nostalgic visual language—soft colors, rounded edges, encouraging copy
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer clarity and efficiency over coziness; design should skew minimal, bright, and task-focused instead

facade.hypothesis: For an anti-cooking app, irreverent humor about kitchen drudgery will feel more authentic than aspirational recipe language
facade.axisTargeted: Friendly vs Professional / Playful vs Serious
facade.label: Sarcastic tone
facade.acceptImplies: The app's voice should lean into self-aware jokes about why cooking sucks, mock the user's kitchen skills, and treat recipes as necessary evils rather than joyful pursuits
facade.rejectImplies: The app should maintain a respectful, encouraging tone that makes cooking feel achievable and positive—even for people who dislike it—avoiding snark that might feel alienating or discouraging

evidence.hypothesis: People intimidated by cooking respond better to a single dominant metric (calories, prep time, or servings) than to dense nutritional tables or ingredient lists.
evidence.implication: Users want information hierarchy—lead with what matters most, bury complexity. Recipe cards should prioritize ONE scannable stat.

facade.hypothesis: For people who hate cooking, removing ingredients from view immediately after selection (rather than showing a staged list) reduces cognitive load and decision fatigue by making the task feel fast and frictionless.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and behavior — instant removal vs. animated list transitions
facade.label: Ingredients vanish instantly
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers snappy, decisive interactions that hide complexity; likely values speed over transparency in the cooking process.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants to see their selections accumulate; prefers fluid, visible transitions that confirm each step and build confidence in the process.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer interfaces that minimize decision-making per interaction — single-action buttons (start, skip, done) over multi-step flows.
facade.label: One-tap cooking
facade.acceptImplies: Design with big, single-purpose buttons and auto-advancing screens; reduce modal dialogs and nested menus.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want more control and visibility — embrace expandable sections, toggles, and inline options even if it adds complexity.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user needs heavy filtering (dietary, time, skill) upfront vs. minimal friction and discovery-based browsing
axes.poleA: Lots of filters or keep it simple

axes.label: Whether user wants smooth, polished animations that feel premium vs. snappy, instant feedback that feels responsive and low-friction

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user needs heavy filtering (dietary, time, skill) upfront vs. minimal friction and discovery-based browsing
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants smooth, polished animations that feel premium vs. snappy, instant feedback that feels responsive and low-friction
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking are more motivated by visual inspiration (photo of finished dish) than by ingredient lists or measurements, which trigger anxiety about precision and effort.
facade.axisTargeted: Information Design — what gets shown vs. what gets hidden
facade.label: Ingredient photos only
facade.acceptImplies: Lead with food porn, bury quantities. Users want to see the *why* (appeal) before the *how* (complexity).
facade.rejectImplies: Users actually need ingredient transparency upfront to feel in control and avoid surprises. List-first design wins.

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers only the easiest, fastest meals with minimal ingredients and steps. Perfect for busy people who want to eat well without the cooking fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite
Meals so easy, you'll actually cook them
Filter by time
Today's easiest meals
Pasta aglio e olio
10 min
3 ingredients
Scrambled eggs on toast
5 min
4 ingredients
Tuna salad sandwich
3 min
5 ingredients
